title: Any of you managed to train a neural network or traditional ML that predicts market movements well?
author: Traditional_Ear5237
selftext: Hey, I have tried to replicate the results of many papers about ML market predictions. They all turned out to be BS. So I am wondering if any of you did manage to train one. Please share your experience and which architecture you used. Thanks.

replies.author: Traditional_Ear5237
replies.body:
I read your post. Actually pretty cool. Very interesting how when people try it they run into very similar learning realizations. 

We did virtually all regular ML and started with RL, DQN did not work on any tests, it has an escaping target so by default it’s an old thing which some new techniques improved upon. DDQN does better, and my favorite is the PPO currently. 

Agreed that complexity does kill, our research shows that no matter the type of network the models like 30-60 features range, more kills results, less kills results. But hey for traditional markets, stocks and what not the ridge regression works quite well. It is extremely simple with just a few betas but yet quite effective and its generalization score is usually on point. 

Simpler almost always better in the end. But I am a big believer that RL is how we are going to achieve something extraordinary in the markets. 

The recent LLM in trading hype is going to make people lose a lot of money.

replies.author: Patient-Bumblebee
replies.body:
PPO and LLM's solve different tasks.

LLM is for code generation, strategy generation, and for running simple strategies that can be described with text.

PPO is for complex black box decision making. 

PPO solved Go. Modern LLM's could generate the code used for solving Go but they couldn't actually solve the game. 

There is a lot of talk in the ML research community about a "RL slump". Is it real? Who knows. But it certainly feels like it.

replies.author: Traditional_Ear5237
replies.body:
Not sure about RL slump. What is it?

And I agree, LLMs can write text.
